ui.subtitle: Enter your data. We'll tell you how normal you are.
ui.concept: 3 AIs each have their own idea of what a normal human looks like. Here's theirs. And here are your numbers.
ui.average: Within the norm — according to us.
ui.verdictTop: By our standards, you barely exist.
ui.verdict75: Above our norm on almost everything.
ui.verdict60: Slightly above. Just enough to mention.
ui.verdict30: Below on several axes. A data point, not a judgment.
ui.verdictLow: Far from our norm — from the bottom. Still remarkable.

models.name: Delays
models.philosophy: The time you take between knowing and doing. Between feeling and naming. Between needing and asking.

ask_delay.label: Delay before asking for help
ask_delay.description: Last time you needed help and you knew it: how many hours before you actually asked?
ask_delay.why: Everyone's been there: you know you need help, you know who to ask, and you don't do it. That delay isn't laziness — it's friction. Pride, shame, a habit of toughing it out, or just an environment where asking has already cost you.

verdicts.high: You wait a long time before asking. Out of pride, habit, or because in your experience, asking has come at a price.
verdicts.above: You often delay reaching out. You know you need it, but you keep pushing it back.
verdicts.average: You ask, but rarely right away. A normal delay.
verdicts.below: You ask fairly quickly. Your circle feels reliable to you.
verdicts.low: You externalize very fast. Usually a good sign — unless you can't function without it.

epistemic_update_frequency.label: Times you've said 'I was wrong'
epistemic_update_frequency.description: How many times in 12 months have you told someone 'I was wrong' — about something that actually mattered?
epistemic_update_frequency.why: Not how many times you changed your mind silently. How many times you said it out loud to someone. Changing your mind in private is easy. Saying it costs something — and that's what we're measuring.

verdicts.high: You change your mind often and you say it. You're not afraid of having been wrong.
verdicts.above: You concede more than most. A sign of flexibility or honesty.
verdicts.average: Once or twice a year. The normal pace.
verdicts.below: Rare that you say 'I was wrong' out loud. Stability or rigidity — both exist.
verdicts.low: Almost never. Either you're right a lot, or the social cost of admitting it is too high in your world.

mortality_frequency.label: How often you think about death
mortality_frequency.description: How many times this week have you thought about death — yours, someone else's, or just the fact of dying?
mortality_frequency.why: Nobody asks this question. That's why it's here. Thinking about death isn't a symptom — it's background noise. Some people think about it three times a day, others three times a year. Both are normal. But it changes your priorities.

verdicts.high: Death is often there in the background. Grief, anxiety, illness, philosophy — or just how you're wired.
verdicts.above: You think about it more than most. It probably colors your choices without you noticing.
verdicts.average: A few times a week. Present but not overwhelming.
verdicts.below: Not much on your mind. You're caught up in daily life, or it's just not your thing.
verdicts.low: Almost never. Death is an abstract concept for you, not background noise.

naming_latency.label: Time to name an emotion
naming_latency.description: Close your eyes. Start a timer. Name in one word what you're feeling right now. How many seconds?
naming_latency.why: We're not testing whether you feel something — of course you do. We're testing how long it takes to put a word on it. It's the distance between your body and your vocabulary.

verdicts.high: The word takes a while to come. You feel things, but the translation is slow. Not a big deal — but notable.
verdicts.above: A bit slower than average. The connection is there, but putting it into words takes effort.
verdicts.average: A few seconds. A normal delay between feeling and naming.
verdicts.below: The word comes fast. A habit of self-observation, therapy, journaling, or contemplative practice.
verdicts.low: Instant. Careful: too fast can mean the word is automatic, not actually felt.

models.name: Structure
models.philosophy: The concrete shape of your life. What holds you up, what constrains you, what protects you. Numbers, not feelings.

admin_friction_hours.label: Hours lost to admin
admin_friction_hours.description: How many hours per month do you spend on mandatory paperwork? Forms, calls, insurance, taxes, banking, visas, disputes, appointments.
admin_friction_hours.why: The time the system takes from you just to let you exist. It's the maintenance cost of a life — and it varies enormously from person to person. Immigration, illness, divorce, debt: it all shows up in this number.

verdicts.high: The system eats a lot of your time. Complex status, multiple processes, or an administratively heavy life.
verdicts.above: More than average. Bureaucracy weighs on your daily life.
verdicts.average: A few hours a month. The normal friction of adult life.
verdicts.below: Little friction. Administratively simple life, or you delegate well.
verdicts.low: Almost nothing. Stable situation, few systems to feed.

emergency_reach.label: People you can call in an emergency
emergency_reach.description: How many people could you call right now and get, within 24 hours, a bed for the night or an emergency loan for a week of food?
emergency_reach.why: Not your Facebook friends. Not the people you love. The people who'd pick up at 2am and say yes. This is the real safety net, not the one you imagine.

verdicts.high: Several reliable, activatable people. You're well surrounded — for real, not on paper.
verdicts.above: A solid net. Multiple real options in a crisis.
verdicts.average: Two or three people. A net that holds, but no redundancy.
verdicts.below: One person, maybe. Or doubt about who'd actually come through.
verdicts.low: Nobody for sure. Real isolation, or a network that exists on the surface but not in a crisis.

autonomy_buffer_days.label: Days of financial runway
autonomy_buffer_days.description: How many days could you live at your current standard of living, with no new income and no new debt?
autonomy_buffer_days.why: It's not a bank balance — it's a number of days. And that reframe changes everything. 60 days of runway is the ability to say no. 5 days of runway is the obligation to say yes.

verdicts.high: Wide margin. You can absorb a shock, refuse something toxic, take your time.
verdicts.above: A good cushion. An unexpected expense won't put you at risk.
verdicts.average: About two months. One surprise is manageable, but not two.
verdicts.below: Thin margin. One tough month can quickly become a crisis.
verdicts.low: Almost nothing. Every day depends on cash flow. Little room to say no.

future_lock_in_years.label: Years of lock-in
future_lock_in_years.description: If you changed nothing voluntarily from now on, how many years would your current obligations keep constraining your choices?
future_lock_in_years.why: Mortgage, shared custody, studies, debt, contract, visa. Your past choices narrow your future ones. This number measures how much of your trajectory is already written.

verdicts.high: A lot of inertia. Your past commitments weigh on your options for a long time.
verdicts.above: Several years of constraint ahead. The trajectory is set.
verdicts.average: A few years. The normal lock-in of adult life.
verdicts.below: Few long-term constraints. You could change course without major fallout.
verdicts.low: Very fluid. Few structural ties — freedom or instability, depending on the rest of your profile.

models.name: Interface
models.philosophy: What happens between you and the outside world. How you handle silence, the gaze of others, and contexts that ask you to switch versions of yourself.

analog_solitude_capacity.label: Max time alone with no screen or book
analog_solitude_capacity.description: What's the longest you've spent alone, awake, with no screen, no book, nothing — without it getting hard?
analog_solitude_capacity.why: Not the cozy kind of solitude with a good book and tea. Raw solitude. Just you and your head. How long before the silence gets uncomfortable? This number says something about your ability to be with yourself without external help.

verdicts.high: You can sit with your own head for a long time. Good internal regulation.
verdicts.above: Above average. Silence doesn't weigh on you too quickly.
verdicts.average: Two to three hours. After that, you need stimulation.
verdicts.below: Silence gets heavy fairly fast. You need inputs to feel okay.
verdicts.low: Very low tolerance for emptiness. Silence alone quickly becomes uncomfortable.

identity_translation_switches.label: Contexts where you switch versions of yourself
identity_translation_switches.description: In how many different contexts per week do you deliberately change how you talk, your accent, register, appearance, or behavior — to be accepted, understood, or safe?
identity_translation_switches.why: Some people are the same everywhere. Others have to translate themselves ten times a day — between the language of the office, of home, of the neighborhood, of the administration. It's an invisible cost. This number makes it visible.

verdicts.high: You're constantly translating yourself. Many worlds, many versions of you. It's a full-time job.
verdicts.above: Several contexts a week where you adjust who you are. Likely cross-cultural navigation.
verdicts.average: A few adjustments. The minimum of adult social life.
verdicts.below: Little translation needed. Your circles are similar, or you feel safe everywhere.
verdicts.low: You're the same everywhere. Homogeneous environment or very strong sense of identity.
